summary: I was born and raised in Cleveland, Ohio, and spent my early childhood there before moving away. Cleveland is where my story begins, and it shaped my early years until I was about eleven. It’s the city where my family roots were planted before we relocated to Michigan.

When I was around eleven years old, my family moved from Cleveland to Michigan. This move marked a new chapter in my life, away from my birthplace. Michigan became my home during my formative years after leaving Ohio.

My German heritage is a big part of my family’s history, especially through my great-great-grandfather Leopold Rothschild and the Adler family in Mannheim, Germany. The story goes back over 120 years to Mannheim, where my ancestors lived before fleeing due to the rise of the Nazi party. Germany is where the Rothschild and Adler families endured World War I and the horrors leading up to Kristallnacht.

Leopold Rothschild was my great-great-grandfather, one of eight Jewish brothers who all served and survived World War I. He lived in Mannheim, Germany, and his family’s story was even featured in a German propaganda newspaper after the war. Leopold’s survival and legacy are central to my understanding of my German-Jewish roots.

World War I deeply affected my family, especially the Rothschild brothers who all served on the front lines and in the trenches. Remarkably, all eight brothers survived the Great War, which was a rare and fortunate outcome. This war shaped the lives of my ancestors in Mannheim and is a key part of our family history.

Selma Rothschild was my great-grandmother, daughter of Leopold Rothschild. She married Hugo Adler, and together they raised my grandmother Marianne and great-uncle Samuel in Mannheim. Selma’s life was deeply intertwined with the Jewish community there before the family fled Germany.

Hugo Adler was my great-grandfather, a Belgian-born German who was very prominent in the Jewish community of Mannheim. He composed music for his synagogue, and his compositions are still used by Jewish organizations worldwide, including in the United States. Hugo married Selma Rothschild, and together they raised my grandmother and great-uncle.

The Jewish faith and community have been central to my family’s identity for generations. From my great-grandfather Hugo Adler’s musical contributions to synagogues to the survival and resilience of my ancestors during times of persecution, Judaism shapes our family story. Even today, my uncles are rabbis and Judaic scholars, continuing this legacy.

Marianne Adler is my grandmother, daughter of Hugo Adler and Selma Rothschild. She grew up in Mannheim but had to flee Germany with her family after Kristallnacht in 1938. Marianne later met and married my grandfather Ronald Aaron in the United States, and they raised my father and uncles together.

Samuel Adler was my great-uncle, the brother of my grandmother Marianne. He was part of the Adler family in Mannheim before they fled Nazi Germany. Samuel’s life, along with Marianne’s, was shaped by the rise of the Nazi party and the family’s eventual emigration to the United States.

The rise of the Nazi Party in Germany disrupted my family’s peaceful life in Mannheim. Their hateful policies and actions, culminating in events like Kristallnacht, forced my great-grandparents and their children to flee for their safety. The Nazi Party’s impact is a dark but crucial part of my family’s history.

Kristallnacht, on November 9, 1938, was a devastating night for my family and the Jewish community in Mannheim. The Mannheim Synagogue, a beautiful cultural hub, was destroyed during this pogrom. After witnessing the destruction, my great-grandfather decided the family had to flee Germany immediately to survive.

The Mannheim Synagogue was a stunning building and a cultural center for the Jews of Mannheim before it was destroyed on Kristallnacht. It held great significance for my family, especially my great-grandfather Hugo Adler, who composed music for it. Seeing it destroyed was a turning point that led my family to escape Germany.

After fleeing Nazi Germany, my family arrived in the United States and settled in Worcester, Massachusetts. The U.S. became the new home where my grandparents built their lives, and where my parents and I were raised. This country represents safety and opportunity for our family after the hardships in Europe.

Worcester, Massachusetts is where my grandparents, Ronald and Marianne Aaron, settled after escaping Germany. It’s where my grandfather was raised by Barney and Gertrude Aaron and where my grandmother joined the family after fleeing Mannheim. Worcester was the starting point for our family’s new life in America.
cues: grandfather raised by Barney and Gertrude

summary: Ronald Aaron is my grandfather, originally born Ronald Aronovitz. He was raised in Worcester by Barney and Gertrude Aaron and had a brother named Hugh. Ronald served in the Air Force, worked as an architect and interior designer, and married my grandmother Marianne after they met in high school.

The Aronovitz family name was the original surname of my grandfather Ronald before it was shortened to Aaron. They were Jewish immigrants from the area between Ukraine and Russia. This name connects us to that Eastern European Jewish heritage before settling in the United States.

My grandfather’s family, originally Aronovitz, came from an area between Ukraine and Russia. This region is part of our Eastern European Jewish heritage. Although we don’t have many details, it’s an important part of where our family’s journey began before coming to America.

Like Ukraine, Russia is part of the region where my grandfather’s family, the Aronovitzes, originated. This area shaped the early history of my Jewish ancestors before they immigrated to the United States. It’s a significant part of our family’s roots in Eastern Europe.

Barney Aaron was my grandfather Ronald’s father, who raised him in Worcester, Massachusetts. Along with my grandmother Gertrude, Barney helped shape Ronald’s upbringing before he served in the military and became an architect. Barney is part of the American chapter of our family story.

Gertrude Aaron was my grandfather Ronald’s mother, who raised him alongside Barney in Worcester. She was part of the family that helped Ronald grow up before his military service and career. Gertrude’s role was central in grounding our family in America.

Hugh Aaron is my grandfather Ronald’s brother. He served in the United States Navy and saw combat in the Philippines during World War II. His military service is an important part of our family’s American legacy.

The Philippines is where my great-uncle Hugh Aaron served in combat as part of the United States Navy during World War II. His service there is a proud part of our family’s military history. It represents the sacrifices made by my family in defense of their new country.

The United States Navy was the branch where my great-uncle Hugh Aaron served during World War II, including combat in the Philippines. His service is a significant part of our family’s contribution to the country. The Navy helped shape his life and legacy.

My grandfather Ronald Aaron served in the United States Air Force after his time in the Navy. He worked on technical blueprints for aircraft, which was a key part of his military career. This service was an important chapter before he pursued his career as an architect and interior designer.

My father and his two brothers were raised in a typical suburban neighborhood in Connecticut. Even though it was a normal American upbringing, they were deeply connected to our Jewish heritage and traditions. Connecticut is where the next generation of our family grew up and embraced Judaism.

Judaism is a cornerstone of my family’s identity, passed down through generations from Germany to America. My great-grandfather Hugo Adler contributed music still used in synagogues, and my uncles are now Judaic scholars and rabbis. Despite growing up in America, my family has always valued and preserved our Jewish faith and traditions.
